{{Short description|Statistical description of identical half-integer spin particles}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Fermi–Dirac statistics
| image = FD e mu.svg
| caption = The Fermi–Dirac distribution for various temperatures. At <math>T = 0</math>, states below the [[Fermi energy]] are fully occupied.
| topics = [[Statistical mechanics]], [[Quantum mechanics]], [[Pauli exclusion principle]], [[Fermion]], [[Spin (physics)]]
}}

'''Fermi–Dirac statistics''' ('''F–D statistics''') is a type of [[quantum statistics]] that applies to systems of [[identical particles|identical]] [[fermion]]s — particles with half-integer [[spin (physics)|spin]] that obey the [[Pauli exclusion principle]]. It was developed by [[Enrico Fermi]] and [[Paul Dirac]] in 1926.

== Fermi–Dirac distribution ==
The average occupation number of a single-particle state with energy <math>\varepsilon</math> is:

:<math>\langle n(\varepsilon) \rangle = \frac{1}{e^{(\varepsilon - \mu)/k_B T} + 1}</math>

where:
* <math>\mu</math> is the [[chemical potential]] ([[Fermi energy]] at <math>T = 0</math>)
* <math>k_B</math> is the [[Boltzmann constant]]
* <math>T</math> is the [[temperature]]

== Properties ==
* At <math>T = 0</math>, all states with <math>\varepsilon < \mu</math> are occupied (<math>\langle n \rangle = 1</math>), all states with <math>\varepsilon > \mu</math> are empty (<math>\langle n \rangle = 0</math>)
* At finite temperature, the distribution near the Fermi level broadens by ~<math>k_B T</math>
* <math>0 \leq \langle n \rangle \leq 1</math> — at most one fermion per state (Pauli exclusion)

== Fermi energy ==
The [[Fermi energy]] <math>E_F</math> is the energy of the highest occupied state at <math>T = 0</math>. For a free [[electron gas]]:

:<math>E_F = \frac{\hbar^2}{2m}(3\pi^2 n)^{2/3}</math>

where <math>n = N/V</math> is the particle density.

== Applications ==
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! System !! Application of F–D statistics
|-
| [[Electron]]s in [[metal]]s || Electrical and thermal conductivity
|-
| [[White dwarf]]s || [[Electron degeneracy pressure]] supports against gravity
|-
| [[Neutron star]]s || Neutron degeneracy pressure
|-
| [[Semiconductor]]s || Carrier statistics, [[Fermi level]] pinning
|-
| [[Proton]]s and [[neutron]]s in nuclei || Nuclear shell model
|}

== Classical limit ==
At high temperatures or low densities (<math>\mu \ll -k_B T</math>), F–D statistics reduces to [[Maxwell–Boltzmann statistics]], the classical limit where quantum effects become negligible.

== Contrast with Bose–Einstein statistics ==
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! !! Fermi–Dirac !! Bose–Einstein
|-
| Spin || Half-integer (fermions) || Integer (bosons)
|-
| Occupation || ≤1 per state || Unlimited per state
|-
| Low-T behaviour || Degeneracy pressure || Bose–Einstein condensation
|}

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Bose–Einstein statistics]]
* [[Fermion]]
* [[Pauli exclusion principle]]
* [[Statistical mechanics]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Statistical mechanics]]
[[Category:Quantum mechanics]]
[[Category:Particle physics]]
